content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Julie Meier Wright 
4778M La Villa Marina 
Marina Del Rey, CA 
90292 
Dear Ms. Wright: 
January 30, 1989 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-88-439 
You have written requesting information regarding two issues 
which arose out of elections in 1988. 
The first issue you raised 
involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign of-
fice rather than to the Registrar of Voters. 
This is an issue 
over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. 
We recommend you 
address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local 
registrar of voters. 
The second issue involved a slate mailer. 
The Commission 
shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with 
slate mailers. 
In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored 
legislation regulating slate mailers. 
(See enclosed manual.) 
That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. 
We are 
continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to 
determine whether further legislation will be needed in the 
future. 
We appreciate the information you have provided to us in 
that regard. 
If you have any questions, or if you would like further 
information, please do not hesitate to contact me. 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
GenQco~~~~ 
BY:\j~~~~ G. McLean 
Counsel, Legal Division 
DMG:JGM:ld 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804 .. 0807 • (916) 322 .. 5660 


November 12, 1988 
Mr. John Larson, Chairman 
Julie Meier Wright 
4778M La Villa Marina 
Marina del Rey, California 90292 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Post Office BOI 807 
Sacramento, CA 9.5804 
Dear Mr. Larson: 
I am not at all knowledgeable about campaign law and I do recognize how 
difficult it is to legislate ethics, but I feel compelled to state my feelings 
about the kind of pOlitical practices that I found particularly repulsive 
during this election cycle. 
Two examples of unethical practices came to light in Santa Monica alone: 
First, the Santa Monica for Renters Rights (SMRR) group conducted an absent 
voter drive where they had absent voter applications returned to a 
campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. The possibilities for 
fraud are clear: all they have to do is selectively forward applications based 
on demographic probabilities whether the voter would be for or against rent 
control. I am certainly hopeful that the FPPC can go after those responsible. 
The second problem was a slate mailer, and I enclose a copy of the mailing (I 
apologize that it is marked up). This mailer is misleading and fraudulent, 
even though it might somehow meet the letter of the law. First, it purports 
to represent Republican candidates, when in fact the candidates listed for the 
school board, the college board, the rent board and the city council would 
never under any circumstances be supported by the Republican Party. Also, 
despite some disclaimers, they use the name "Victory '88" as the 
organization behind the mailing. "Victory '88" was used throughout the 
election by the Republican National Committee in their mailings -- clearly 
this was an intent to mislead. They also indicate that those candidates 
marked with an asterisk helped to finance the mailing; I understand second-
hand that in at least one case this is not true. It's pretty clear that insurance 
companies supporting proposition 101 were the catalyst behind this mailer 


Mr. John Larson 
November 12, 1988 
Page Two 
-- just another example of the extremes the insurance companies will go to 
in an attempt to protect their lucrative business in California. 
Perhaps outlawing all slate mailers is one solution to this kind of unethical 
mailing. 
Because of the misleading nature of the advertising on so many of the issues, 
I think: it would also be good to require all campaign advertising to carry the 
formal name of the proposition on file with the Secretary of State, if not 
more descriptive information. 
If there is anything that I, as a citizen, can do to tighten up the laws on 
campaign advertising and direct mail, I will be delighted to do it. If you 
have any summary information on regulations affecting this kind of 
campaign effort, I would be interested in having it. 
Thanks for listening. 
Sincerely, 
cc: John Greenwood, Legislative Coordinator

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Julie Meier Wright 
4778M La Villa Marina 
Marina del Rey, CA 
90292 
Dear Ms. Wright: 
November 18, 1988 
Re: 
88-439 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on November 17, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329).} 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
1-( '" 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916)322,5660 


November 12, 1988 
Mr. John Larson, Chairman 
Julie Meier Wright 
4778M La Villa Marina 
Marina del Rey, California 90292 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street. Suite 800 
Post Office BOI 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804

Nov II 8 37 M1 'BB

• First, the Santa Monica for Renters Rights (SMRR) group conducted an absent 
voter drive where they had absent voter applications returned to a 
campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. The possibilities for 
fraud are clear: all they have to do is selectively forward applications based 
on demographic probabilities whether the voter would be for or against rent 
control. I am certainly hopeful that the FPPC can go after those responsible. 
The second problem was a slate mailer, and I enclose a copy of the mailing (I 
apologize that it is marked up). This mailer is misleading and fraudulent, 
even though it might somehow meet the letter of the law. First, it purports 
to represent Republican candidates, when in fact the candidates listed for the 
school board, the college board, the rent board and the city council would 
never under any circumstances be supported by the Republican Party. Also. 
despite some disclaimers, they use the name "Victory '88" as the 
organization behind the mailing. "Victory '88" was used throughout the 
election by the Republican National Committee in their mailings -- clearly 
this was an intent to mislead. They also indicate that those candidates 
marked with an asterisk helped to fmance the mailing; I understand second-
hand that in at least one case this is not true. It's pretty clear that insurance 
companies supporting proposition 101 were the catalyst behind this mailer 


Mr. john Larson 
November 12, 1988 
Page Two 
-- just another example of the extremes the insurance companies will go to 
in an attempt to protect their lucrative business in California. 
Perhaps outlawing aU slate mailers is one solution to this kind of unethical 
mailing. 
Because of the misleading nature of the advertising on so many of the issues, 
I think it would also be good to require aU campaign advertising to carry the 
formal name of the proposition on file with the Secretary of State, if not 
more descriptive information. 
If there is anything that I, as a citizen, can do to tighten up the laws on 
campaign advertiSing and direct mail, I will be delighted to do it. If you 
have any summary information on regulations affecting this kind of 
• campaign effort, I would be interested in having it. 
Thanks for listening. 
cc: John Greenwood, Legislative Coordinator

sections.conclusion: The first issue involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. This is an issue over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local registrar of voters. The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored legislation regulating slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.) That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to determine whether further legislation will be needed in the future.
sections.question_synthetic: Julie Meier Wright submitted complaints regarding two election-related practices: (1) absentee voter applications being returned to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters, and (2) a slate mailer that she believed was misleading and fraudulent in its representation of candidates and use of the 'Victory '88' organization name.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined jurisdiction over the absentee voter application issue and recommended addressing it to the Secretary of State or local registrar. Regarding slate mailers, the FPPC acknowledged the concerns and noted that 1987 legislation regulating slate mailers became effective January 1, 1988, with the Commission continuing to monitor such activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of multiple pieces: incoming complaint letter (Nov 12, 1988), FPPC acknowledgment letter (Nov 18, 1988), and formal FPPC response letter (Jan 30, 1989). Slate mailer sample document included. Classified as 'correspondence' due to multi-part nature; primary FPPC response extracted. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but substantive advice provided in response letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Julie Meier Wright submitted complaints regarding two election-related practices: (1) absentee voter applications being returned to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters, and (2) a slate mailer that she believed was misleading and fraudulent in its representation of candidates and use of the 'Victory '88' organization name.

CONCLUSION: The first issue involved returning absentee voter applications to a campaign office rather than to the Registrar of Voters. This is an issue over which the Commission has no jurisdiction. We recommend you address your concern to the Secretary of State or your local registrar of voters. The second issue involved a slate mailer. The Commission shares your concern over potential abuses which may occur with slate mailers. In 1987, the Commission successfully sponsored legislation regulating slate mailers. (See enclosed manual.) That legislation became effective on January 1, 1988. We are continuing to monitor slate mailing activities in order to determine whether further legislation will be needed in the future.

embedding.summary: This file contains Julie Meier Wright's November 1988 complaint letter to the FPPC regarding two questionable election practices in Santa Monica, followed by the FPPC's acknowledgment letter and formal response declining jurisdiction over the absentee voter application issue while addressing her slate mailer concerns.
